content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
SUPERSEDED
Practices Commission
2.1 (a )C L/)
June 10,
1994
Honorable Helen Fisicaro
Councilmember, City of Coma
Honorable Dennis Fisicaro
Councilmember, City of Colma
490
D Street
Colma,
CA
94014
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94-178
Dear Councilmembers:
You have requested advice concerning the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Wehave
previously provided advice in the form of informal assistance to
Councilmember Dennis Fisicaro in the Cornell Advice Letter,
No.
1-91—212, regarding a similar set of facts.
In the Cornell Advice Letter,
supra,
we concluded that
payments received by firefighter volunteers of the Colma Fire
Protection District are governmental salary.
Thus, the payments
would not create a conflict of interest with respect to Colma City
Council decisions affecting the Colma Fire Protection District.
This letter confirms that the advice is still applicable.
In addition, please note that our advice is limited to the
Political Reform Act.
You may wish to contact the Attorney
General’s office with respect to other provisions of the law such
as Government Code 1090 and the law governing incompatible
offices.
QUESTION
As members of the Colma City Council,
may you participate in
city council decisions which may affect the Colma Fire Protection
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California ‘Code of. Regulations, Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-178
Page
2
District where Councilmember Dennis Fisicaro is a volunteer
firefighter compensated for services on a per—call basis?
CONCLUSION
Salary and reimbursernent for expenses or per diem received
from a state, local, orThderal government agency is exempt from
the definition of “income” for purposes of the Act.
Moreover,
local government agencies are not considered “business entities”
under the Act.
Thus, neither one of you will have a conflict of
interest by virtue of Councilmember Dennis Fisicaro’s employment
with the Colma Fire Protection District.
However, neither one of you may participate in any decision
that will materially affect your personal expenses,
income,
assets, or liabilities by $250 or more.
FACTS
2
You are both incumbent city councilmembers for the City of
Coma.
You are also each other’s spouse.
Councilmember Dennis Fisicaro is also a volunteer firefighter.
for the Colma Fire Protection District (the “district”).
The
district is controlled by an elected coirunission and is staffed by
volunteers.
The district’s firefighter volunteers receive no salary but
do receive compensation for each actual call.
There are some
additional minor benefits,
such as dinners at the firehouse.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
A “public official” is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural
person who is a member, officer,
employee,
or consultant of a
state or local government agency.
This definition would include
members of the Colma City Council.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
2
The facts concerning Councilmemer Dennis Fisicaro are
identical to those set forth in the Cornell Advice Letter,
supra.
However, that letter also discussed the responsibilities of
Councilmember Ray Boudewyn under conflict—of--interest provisions
of the Act.


Our File No. A-94-178
Page
3
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
3 or on:
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to tile public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(c)
and
(d)
(emphasis added).
According to your facts, while the volunteer firefighters
with the Colma Fire Protection District are not considered regular
“employees” of the district, they receive compensation for each
call as they are needed.
Moreover, they receive meals at the
firehouse while on call.
In the Cornell Advice Letter, surä,
we
advised that the payments received by the firefighters are in the
form of governmental salary and do not create a conflict of
interest with respect to decisions affecting the fire district.
(Section 82030(b) (2)
and Section 87103(c).)
We relied on the reasoning in the Jenke Advice Letter,
No.
A-83-001, which concerned employees of a public hospital where
the hospital could arrange for physician services on a part—time
or as—needed basis.
There,
we advised that in the case of a small
hospital,
payments received by a doctor under this arrangement
were salary from a local governmental entity,
subject to the
exclusion under subdivision
(b) (2)
of Section 82030.
Section 82030(b) (2)
expressly exempts salary from a state,
local, or federal government agency from the definition of
“income” for purposes of the Act.
(Also see,
Maness Advice
Letter,
No.
A—93-282; Morita Advice Letter,
No.
1—93—053; Fatland
Advice Letter No.
1-89-419.)
Thus,
on the basis of the reasoning
applied in the Jenke Advice Letter,
supra,
and as previously
advised in the Cornell Advice Letter,
supra, the payments
Councilmember Fisicaro receives as a volunteer firefighter are
salary from a local government agency and are excluded from the
definition of “income” under the Act.
“Immediate family” means the spouse and dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No. A-94-178
Page
4
Thus,
Councilmember Dennis Fisicaro’s salary from the
district does not create a conflict of interest for either of you
with respect to city decisions affecting the district.
(Sampson
Advice Letter, No. 1-89-196.)
In addition, because a local
government agency is not an organization or enterprise operated
for profit, it is not a “business entity” as defined by the Act.
(Section B2005.)
However,
a conflict of interest may still exist where a
decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets, or
liabilities of a public official or his or her iiiuuediate family
increasing or decreasing by $250, irrespective of the source of
the increase or decrease.
(Regulation 18702 • 1(a) (4); Torrance
Advice Letter, No. 1-89-142.)
Thus, for example, this provision
contemplates even increases and decreases to income received from
governmental agencies as being within the parameters of the
regulation.
Therefore, you may be disqualified from participating
in city decisions which would have a direct personal effect on
either one of you.
This might be the case, for example, if a city decision
involved the hiring, firing, promotion, demotion, or discipline of
Counc±lmember Dennis Fisicaro as a firefighter, or the setting of
a salary for the councilmember which is different from salaries
paid to other employees of the fire district.
(Regulation
18702.1(c).)
It would appear that it is unlikely that the city
would be involved in these fire district decisions, however.
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested.
If you have further questions regarding this matter,
please contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel,
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
